title: Hilda Kibet -Director's Interview - Megan | Managing Editor
meeting_title: Hilda Kibet -Director's Interview - Megan | Managing Editor

transcript.text: Thank you.
Hi, Hilda.
Hi, Megan.
Nice to meet you.
Good to meet you, too.
Yeah, where are you based?
Nairobi, Kenya.
Oh, nice.
Awesome.
Cool.
I've been there once before.
Yeah, it's been a few years, though.
That's lovely.
Yeah, but I'm based here in San Francisco.
So, yeah, it's really good to meet you.
Yeah, so just to sort of, I know you've already had a few conversations with other folks on the team, but yeah, just a little bit about me.
So I'm a director here at GrowthX.
I help manage client accounts and work pretty closely with managing editors on the team.

And yeah, so for this part of the interview process, I'm really just like hoping to understand a little bit more about like how you execute and like manage multiple accounts and like quality control and things like that.
Yeah, sure.
Cool.
Awesome.
Yeah.
Yeah, so would you be able to describe a time when you were managing multiple projects or accounts with competing deadlines and how you organized your work?
Yeah, so I've done that in several roles during the duration of my career.
One particular time I'd like to...
Call-out is when I was managing editor at Real and Limited Agency.
So we had multiple clients and our delivery timelines or brand, the way the brand worked was we are an agency that delivers within 24 hours or 48 hours.
So clients would order work by number of words and we had deadlines depending on the number of words.
So for instance, you can subscribe to a subscription that delivers 1200 words in 24 hours.
So we had so many such clients and my role was to ensure that whatever content request we received went through the entire pipeline from strategy to writing to editorial to delivery within 24 hours.
However, the best way to do that was not to compact it into 24 hours.
We'd receive batch orders from them.

So I'd be able to plan ahead of time, assign it to the writers, and then by the time we are at the 24-hour mark, it's with the editor, and then it would be handed over to me for the final review and then to deliver it to the client.
The client had an unlimited amount of time to request feedback, so once we delivered that first part, we'd be able to move on to the next as we wait for feedback.
And then within the first few weeks of onboarding a client, I was very keen on the feedback they provided, and I would add this to the briefs for writers and to provide it to the editor as well.
So that we were generating content that is almost 100% from the writer stage, and that improved our delivery time.
So this was very manual, there was no AI, only Grammarly, and...
SEMrush, so yeah, which are still AI tools, yeah, but so a lot of it was still manual at the time, but that's all.

Yeah, yeah, and you mentioned, yeah, you mentioned, like, using Grammarly and SEMrush, but I guess, yeah, I'm curious about, like, what other tools you used to actually sort of, like, kind of manage and, like, and track where the content was in each phase.
So the agency itself used us, had their own inbuilt software, which I would say was not as ideal as using a project management tool, like Asana, for instance.
Yeah, so we would, the way I'd manage the work is, there was a complete transparency between me and the client, so there was a way, like, would I'd manage it on one page.
So whenever we'd receive a request, all the conversations...
What happened within that page?
And that is how the software was built.
It was not a project management software, but how I managed the team was, I managed the team, I trained the team, I built Notion templates, Notion training templates.

So the team was aware of our structure and our processes, and I improved this over time, especially in the beginning, because different writers were in different time zones, and different clients were also, mostly US-based, but we had some plans in the UK as well, and Canada.
So I built out a lot of documentation in Notion to support the writers, to support the editors, and to support the team in understanding our processes and our delivery timelines, and I improved this.
And I also created a platform where the team could provide feedback and could engage.
So this helped us to collaborate, but at remote, we use Asana.
And I have a person.
I worked in the beginning in building a project, a project, automations for one of the projects, content projects we were working on.
And so I was able to add, you know, to track the projects by adding the automation site that when a writer completes, it goes to an editor automatically, it goes to me.
And then we were able to collaborate with the production manager, and we were able to publish on time.
So I worked with Asana as well, even in previous roles.
Okay, great.
Awesome.
Yeah.
Seems like you have all the tools and can use them well.
So yeah, that's great.
Okay, and then, so yeah, just a little bit on like quality control now.
Would you be able to tell me a time where like you caught a major issue with content before it shipped to the customer?
And, like, what was it that you caught?
And then how did you catch it?
Yeah, so one thing that I caught with so many new writers when I was managing editor was there was a lot of solid content, but a lot of time it lacked strategic alignment with a business goal.
So it's something I also experienced when I was starting out.
And so I knew how to guide the writers to align it with a business goal.
And once I would work, I would rework the piece myself, and then I'd go back to train and show the before and after to update the brief.
So I added a section in the brief that, you know, I showed that, you know, you have to align this piece with this, whether it's engagement, whether we are trying to rank it.
And something also about the work that we are delivering, all SEO.
We'll back
The that we would deliver would provide an SEO score.
So we would run it through RFs and then we would provide the SEO score to the client during the delivery of the content.
So I trained the writers in such a way that they knew how to align the content with the business.
Because, again, with an agency, you deal with all types of businesses.
So, yeah, it was helpful to add that to the brief.
Okay, nice.
Perfect.

Yeah, and then would you be able to talk about a time when you maybe received, like, critical feedback from a leader or a boss or even from a client and what the feedback was and then how you responded?
Yeah, yeah.
So I did, again, on the same issue of aligning content early on in my career.
And it's something that made me really work on that.
I received...

Feedback from my manager that my work was solid, it was flowing well, it was accurate grammatically, the keywords were there, but they felt like it was not aligning with the goals that that particular client wanted.

So I went back to, I worked with my manager to understand how to align, what metrics should I look at, what a piece of content that is aligned, that is for educational should look like, what a piece of content that we are trying to rank looks like.

So I worked on, you know, building my knowledge and skills in those areas, and it has helped me over the course of my career as an editor as well, and it has helped me to also train other people on the same, yeah.
Yeah, thanks, cool, okay, great.
Yeah, I mean, those were like my main questions.
But I also just want to see if you have any questions for me, or if there's anything that you just want to make sure I know about you and your experience.
Yeah, I have some questions, actually, wrote them down.
Yeah, for sure.
Yeah, so what's one win you would want me to deliver that you would say, you know, this was a great hire within, you know, the first few months?
Oh, wait, sorry.
So, like, what would it, sorry, could you just repeat that?
I think I missed the beginning.
Yeah, what's one win that you would want?
okay.
Yeah, yeah, for sure.

I think, like, when, yeah, when we think about this role, it's like, ultimately, we want someone who is, like, very editorial, editorially savvy, but also very technically capable, because we use, like, we use our, like, AI workflows, like, we're a very, like, AI first company, and

And, like, and, like, and it sounds like you've, like, also just created processes, like, using, like, certain tools, like, whether it's, like, and Notion or Asana and just, like, essentially, like, improving upon processes and, like, that, like, documentation.
Like, I think that those are some, those would be some key wins.
It's, like, it's, like, being able to produce that quality content and ultimately just, like, make the client happy.
Because, like, every, depending on the client, like, you might be doing anywhere from, like, five blog posts a week for them up to maybe, like, 20 a week.

It depends on the client and those numbers will also sort of change as we start to renew some clients and then they'll, like, that one at, like, 20, 20 blog posts a week would likely be moved down to, like, a five a week just because we realized that.

Like, we kind of have struggled with some of that quality, like the more posts we're doing, because it's just harder to, even though like AI is doing the bulk of it, like we still need humans to actually review it.
And there's just only so much time of the day.
But yeah, does that answer your question?
I feel like it was maybe a little bit, but just, yeah.
Yeah, I think you've answered my question in the sense that you helped me understand what is the expectation of the role.
Yeah, and I, yeah, because I think also our, like this role, I think is also changing as we speak.
Like, we're in the midst of a bit of a reorg.

And so, whereas before, like managing editors would have, whereas before, like we had content managers, and these content managers were the ones working in the AI workflows, and then sending that for edits to the managing editor.
know.
But now… now…
Now what we're looking for is just actually having the managing editor handle that end-to-end production.
So the managing editor is going to be working in the AI workflows, generating the content, but then also being responsible for editing and polishing that content.
So that's why we need folks who are highly, highly capable on the editorial side and don't need as much oversight.

And that's why, yeah, so that's why I think just like, it all comes down to just like quality content and making sure that like, yeah, just like you said, it flows well, but also it needs that strategic intent.
Because that's also something that is obviously like very, very important to our clients.
So what's something you're excited about currently in how the team works, in where growthx, the team is going?
Yeah, I mean, it's, it's a pretty exciting time.
Like, so I've been at the company.

company just since May of this year, and when I first joined, we were actually using an old, we were using this other workflow, but now since I've joined, we've actually built our own in-house AI workflows, and then we're also gearing up to release our own analytics platform.
So just like, I feel like the tech side of things is really kind of like, catching up to like, our like, client delivery operations.
It's like, we're trying to be like, tech first versus like, services first.
So I think that's been cool to witness just like seeing the, just seeing like the capabilities of our, of our tools actually evolve in real time.
And even just like, like, I think also something that's going to be important in this role is like, the engineering team, they're always open to feedback.
They want our feedback.
They want people to kind of like test and play around the tool and tell them where they think it can be improved upon.

So like we kind of need that like tinkering mindset of just like, you know, like I'm not I'm maybe not quite getting the right output from this tool right now, but like maybe I can like make some tweaks and like maybe like I have to like adjust the prompts or like adjust the artifacts and and then compare the the output and just kind of like have just like constantly iterate upon upon what's there.
yeah, I was actually telling Panzer during my interview that I think that would be one of the most exciting parts of this.
Yeah, yeah, it's fun.

Yeah, yeah, I've been involved in almost a similar role at remote where I've been, there's the campaign engine that we were trying to build and so I was part of it from the onset of and I want that the process of iterating.
right.
Yeah.
Yeah.
mean, yeah,
Mapping out bottlenecks, improving it.
So it's been so much fun to work on that.
And I think that would be one of the exciting parts because it's like something different from just normal editorial work.
It's like technical.
So it adds to the role and it's important.
Yeah, absolutely.
Yeah.
Something else that I wanted to ask, if you still have time.
Yeah, yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah, so you mentioned that you're working with clients directly and account managers.
So how does this team currently measure success?
Yeah, so a big emphasis for us right now is on renewals.

But I think in terms of like on like a day-to-day, week-to-week, month-to-month basis, it's like, so at the beginning of every engagement with a client, we're resetting the goals for what we're We're wanting to see.

So it's like, we're, in our weekly calls with clients, we're doing performance reporting, like, looking at SERP rankings, looking at, like, overall, like, clicks and impressions, but then also looking at LLM referrals, just because, like, so much of, there's just now so much more emphasis on, like, how are our clients actually surfacing in, like, Cloud and ChatGPT, et cetera.

So, so those to, like, we're constantly tracking those, but then in terms of, like, so those are kind of, like, wins for the client, but then wins for us as a company, it's, it's really about, like, renewals and, like, making sure that, like, we're continuing to provide enough value to our clients to make them want to keep working with us.
Oh, yeah, I think in agencies, generally, that's something that is critical.
Yeah.
Retention and renewal.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
So, okay, about your experience, how has it been working at GrowthX?
What has changed and where do you see it going in the near future?
Yeah, for sure.
Yeah, so like I said, yeah, I've been here since May and like my background was actually in journalism.
Like I spent like almost 15 years as a journalist, like covering like technology and then like local news as well.
And so like this has also been, it's definitely been like a bit of a change for me.

But like I think the, I think, yeah, the coolest part is just kind of like what we talked about a little bit earlier is just like being able to really like get deep in sort of like the technical side of things.
Because that wasn't really something I was able to do in previous jobs.
But like I think in, yeah, but I think also just in terms of like the direction where the company is going, like, yeah, like moving toward being like, like.
a tech platform.
like, I think, and again, like, this is kind of like a work in progress.
But I think right now, like the idea is that eventually, like, we'll be able to offer two products.
So like, there's going to be like, maybe if people, if clients just want like, a self serve type of experience, where like, they don't necessarily like have like an account manager, etc.
And like, they're just like, and they're just like, oh, maybe we'll just actually do our own content, but just like using the product that we use internally.
So that could be one side.
But then the other side is like, if clients are still wanting more like, hands on expertise, and wanting someone else to do the work for them, then like, that's the other option.
And so, because like, there are definitely like some clients that I can imagine, like, would, would want to just be on that more like self serve side.
Oh, yeah, like offering the engine to force for
Okay, that's really interesting.
Yeah, that's really interesting.
I think those are all the questions I have.
Okay.
Yeah.
Okay, great.
Well, yeah, it was really great chatting.
And yeah, the next step is like a little like take-home assignment.
But Murilia, we'll be in touch with you about that.
Thank you.
Thank you so much.
Is there something you would want me to clarify before you leave?
No, no, I think I'm all set.
Yeah, just really thank you for your time.
Yeah, was great chatting.
Okay.
Thank you for your time too.
Have a great day.
Yeah, you too.
All right.
Thanks a little bit.
Bye.
Bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Interviewing Hilda Kibet for the Managing Editor role.](https://fathom.video/share/qm1GRZPzBWixQGTmhC4Y7h_rCp98TPdj?tab=summary&timestamp=660.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**Experience:** Kibet managed high-volume, 24/48-hr content delivery at Real and Limited Agency, using proactive batching and Notion documentation to maintain quality under tight deadlines.](https://fathom.video/share/qm1GRZPzBWixQGTmhC4Y7h_rCp98TPdj?tab=summary&timestamp=696.0)
  - [**Critical Skill:** Identified and solved a key quality issue: content lacking strategic alignment with client business goals. This led to a process change—adding a "business goal" section to all briefs—to prevent recurrence.](https://fathom.video/share/qm1GRZPzBWixQGTmhC4Y7h_rCp98TPdj?tab=summary&timestamp=1011.0)
  - [**Role Evolution:** The Managing Editor role is expanding to include end-to-end production (AI generation + editing), requiring strong editorial skills and a technical "tinkering mindset" to improve GrowthX's in-house AI tools.](https://fathom.video/share/qm1GRZPzBWixQGTmhC4Y7h_rCp98TPdj?tab=summary&timestamp=1347.0)
  - [**Company Direction:** GrowthX is transitioning from a services-first agency to a tech-first platform, with plans for both a self-serve product and a hands-on service model.](https://fathom.video/share/qm1GRZPzBWixQGTmhC4Y7h_rCp98TPdj?tab=summary&timestamp=1729.0)

## Topics

### Candidate Experience & Process Management

  - [**Context:** Managed high-volume, 24/48-hour content delivery at Real and Limited Agency.](https://fathom.video/share/qm1GRZPzBWixQGTmhC4Y7h_rCp98TPdj?tab=summary&timestamp=710.0)
  - [**Process:** Proactively batched client orders to create a buffer, ensuring content was with editors by the 24-hour mark.](https://fathom.video/share/qm1GRZPzBWixQGTmhC4Y7h_rCp98TPdj?tab=summary&timestamp=771.0)
  - [**Onboarding:** Used initial client feedback to refine briefs, aiming for \~100% quality from the first draft to improve efficiency.](https://fathom.video/share/qm1GRZPzBWixQGTmhC4Y7h_rCp98TPdj?tab=summary&timestamp=809.0)
  - [**Tooling:**](https://fathom.video/share/qm1GRZPzBWixQGTmhC4Y7h_rCp98TPdj?tab=summary&timestamp=852.0)
      - [**Notion:** Built templates and documentation to standardize processes for a remote team.](https://fathom.video/share/qm1GRZPzBWixQGTmhC4Y7h_rCp98TPdj?tab=summary&timestamp=903.0)
      - [**Asana:** Created automations (e.g., writer → editor handoff) to streamline workflows.](https://fathom.video/share/qm1GRZPzBWixQGTmhC4Y7h_rCp98TPdj?tab=summary&timestamp=952.0)
      - [**SEMrush:** Used for SEO scoring, which was delivered to clients with the final content.](https://fathom.video/share/qm1GRZPzBWixQGTmhC4Y7h_rCp98TPdj?tab=summary&timestamp=1075.0)

### Quality Control & Strategic Alignment

  - [**Problem:** New writers produced technically sound content that lacked strategic alignment with client business goals (e.g., engagement, ranking).](https://fathom.video/share/qm1GRZPzBWixQGTmhC4Y7h_rCp98TPdj?tab=summary&timestamp=1011.0)
  - [**Solution:**](https://fathom.video/share/qm1GRZPzBWixQGTmhC4Y7h_rCp98TPdj?tab=summary&timestamp=1027.0)
    1.  [**Demonstrate:** Reworked a sample piece to show the "before and after" of strategic alignment.](https://fathom.video/share/qm1GRZPzBWixQGTmhC4Y7h_rCp98TPdj?tab=summary&timestamp=1027.0)
    2.  [**Standardize:** Added a mandatory "business goal" section to all content briefs.](https://fathom.video/share/qm1GRZPzBWixQGTmhC4Y7h_rCp98TPdj?tab=summary&timestamp=1048.0)
    3.  [**Train:** Used the reworked piece as a training example for the team.](https://fathom.video/share/qm1GRZPzBWixQGTmhC4Y7h_rCp98TPdj?tab=summary&timestamp=1048.0)
  - [**Origin:** This focus on strategic alignment was a direct result of critical feedback from a manager earlier in Kibet's career.](https://fathom.video/share/qm1GRZPzBWixQGTmhC4Y7h_rCp98TPdj?tab=summary&timestamp=1110.0)

### GrowthX's Vision & Role Expectations

  - [**Role Evolution:** The Managing Editor role now includes end-to-end production, from AI generation to final editing.](https://fathom.video/share/qm1GRZPzBWixQGTmhC4Y7h_rCp98TPdj?tab=summary&timestamp=1347.0)
      - [**Rationale:** Requires highly capable editors who need minimal oversight.](https://fathom.video/share/qm1GRZPzBWixQGTmhC4Y7h_rCp98TPdj?tab=summary&timestamp=1379.0)
  - [**Company Transition:** Shifting from a services-first agency to a tech-first platform.](https://fathom.video/share/qm1GRZPzBWixQGTmhC4Y7h_rCp98TPdj?tab=summary&timestamp=1729.0)
      - [**Tech Development:** Building in-house AI workflows and a new analytics platform.](https://fathom.video/share/qm1GRZPzBWixQGTmhC4Y7h_rCp98TPdj?tab=summary&timestamp=1434.0)
      - [**Product Vision:** Eventually offer two models: a self-serve platform and a hands-on service.](https://fathom.video/share/qm1GRZPzBWixQGTmhC4Y7h_rCp98TPdj?tab=summary&timestamp=1746.0)
  - [**Required Mindset:** A "tinkering mindset" is essential for providing feedback to the engineering team and iterating on AI prompts to improve tool output.](https://fathom.video/share/qm1GRZPzBWixQGTmhC4Y7h_rCp98TPdj?tab=summary&timestamp=1489.0)

### Measuring Success

  - [**Primary Metric:** Client renewals.](https://fathom.video/share/qm1GRZPzBWixQGTmhC4Y7h_rCp98TPdj?tab=summary&timestamp=1595.0)
  - [**Client-Facing Metrics:**](https://fathom.video/share/qm1GRZPzBWixQGTmhC4Y7h_rCp98TPdj?tab=summary&timestamp=1595.0)
      - [SERP rankings, clicks, and impressions.](https://fathom.video/share/qm1GRZPzBWixQGTmhC4Y7h_rCp98TPdj?tab=summary&timestamp=1606.0)
      - [LLM referrals (e.g., surfacing in Claude, ChatGPT).](https://fathom.video/share/qm1GRZPzBWixQGTmhC4Y7h_rCp98TPdj?tab=summary&timestamp=1606.0)
  - [**Internal Goal:** Optimize production volume (e.g., 5-20 posts/week) to balance client needs with maintaining high quality.](https://fathom.video/share/qm1GRZPzBWixQGTmhC4Y7h_rCp98TPdj?tab=summary&timestamp=1290.0)

## Next Steps

  - [**Murilia:** Contact Hilda Kibet with a take-home assignment.](https://fathom.video/share/qm1GRZPzBWixQGTmhC4Y7h_rCp98TPdj?tab=summary&timestamp=1811.0)

action_items.description: Email Hilda Kibet re: take-home assignment details
